ISSUES AND ANALYSIS
1. Do PERS board members, officers or employees manage
"public investments" erithin the neaninq of the term as used in
Section 872 00?
In a staff memorandum issued March 22 , ].990, we construed
the term trpublic investmentstr to include investments made with
ttpubl j-c moneys. rr You suggest that the term ttpubl-ic noneysrr does
not include nonies held and invested by PERS, because the funds
rnanacred bv PERS are trust funds pursuant to both the California
consfitution3 and Section 2o2oo1 . You state that these funds are
composed of ernployer and enpl-oyee contributions and the interest
thereon and are not public moneys or part of the general fund.
our description of public j-nvestroents in the March 22
memorandum as including 'rpublic moneysrt was intended to encompass
a wide spectrum of funds within the anbit of the term. A
statutory definition of trpublic moneysrr is set forth in Penal code
Section 425.5 Typically, that section has been broadly
interpreted to include funds received by or held by a public of-
ficial in his official 
capacity. (center For Publj'c rnterest Law
v. Fair Political Practices Commission 210 cal. App' 3d 1476
(1989); People v. Crosbv, 141 Ca1 . APp.2d L72 (7956),' PeoDIe v.
dest 172 cal. App.2d 692 (1959).) 
It is the official 
character
3 Arti"1" xvl, Section 17(a) of the California Constitution
provides as follows:
The assets of a public pension or retirement systern
are trust funds and shall be held for the exclusive
purposes of providing benefi,ts to participants in the
pension or retirement systems and their beneficiaries
and defraying reasonable expenses of adrninistering the
systern .
4 section 2o2oo provides, in pertinent part, as follows:
The Public Employees' Retirement Funds is a trust
fund created, and adninistered in accordance with the
provisions of this part, soIely for the benefit of the
rnenbers and retired menbers of the systen and their
survivors and beneficiaries.
5 th"t section defines trpublic moneys,tt in pertinent part, as
fol-Iows:
...tA]11 bonds and evidence of indebtedness, and
all moneys belonginq to the state, or any city, county,
town, district, 
or public agency therein, and all
moneys, bonds, and evidences of indebtedness received or
held by state, county, district, 
city, town, or publi.c
agency officers in their official, capacity.


in which the funds are recej-ved, rather than its ultirnate owner-
shj-p, which characterizes the funds as pubJ-ic moneys. (People v.
Crosbv, supra. )
...Pena1 code section 426's definition of rrpublic
noneysrr is phrased in inclusive language so the defini-
tion is net if the rnoney either belongs to the state g!
is received or held by a state officer acting in his or
her official 
capacity. 
(Emphasis in original .)
Center For Public Interest Lawr SlfEEq at 1481.
PERS is a state agency. Its board members, officers and
employees hold and invest the state public employees' retirement
trust funds. Even if the nonies are not characterized as belong-
ing to the state, they are nevertheless received or held by offic-
era of the state acting in their official 
capacity. Therefore, we
would characterize the retirement trust funds adrninistered by PERS
as trpubl ic moneys . rl
In any event, we believe that the definition of trpublic
i.nvestmentsrr for purposes of Section 87200 should be construed to
include noney held in public employee retirement trust funds.
The Act should be liberalLy construed to accornplish its purposes.
(Section 81003.) one of the purposes of the Act is to assure
disclosure of assets and incorne of public officials 
which may be
materially affected by their official 
actions in order that
conflicts of interest may be avoided. (Section 81002(c).) The
thrust of the new provision of Section 87200 is to assure that
pubtic officials 
who are in charqe.of investing funds held by a
lub1ic agency are subject to the discLosure and disqualif ication
provisions of the Act.
In order to clarify the application of the term t'public
investmentsrr for purposes of Section 872oo, staff is recomrnending
that any regulation adopted to clarify the section specifically
refer to pullic employee retirement funds. This should mininize
confusion as to the scope of application.

Do Sections 89503, 89505 and 89505 applv to individuals

(a) No member of a state board or commission, and

would be required to report the receipt of incorne or
gifts from that source on his or her statement of
economic interest. 
This section shall not apply to any
part-tine member of the governing board of any public
institution of higher education, unless the rnernber is
also an elected official.
(b) Subdivisions (b), (c), (d), and (e) of Section
89502 shall apply to this section.
(c) Every state agency shall amend its conflict of
interest code in accordance with this section no later
than December 31, 1991. (Emphasis added.)
section 895o5 provides as follows:
(a) No member of a state board or commission- and
no desiqnated enplovee of a state aqencv, sha1l accept
gifts with a total value of more than tr.ro hundred fifty
dollars ($250) in a calendar year from any single
source, if the member or enPloyee would be required to
report the receipt of income or gifts from that source
on his or her statement of economic interest. 
This
paragraph shalI not apply to any Part-tirne menber of the
qoveining board of any public institution of higher
education, unless the member is also an elected of-
ficial. 
(Emphasis added. )
Section 89506 provides as foIlor^rs:
(a) Payments, advances, or reirnbursements for
travel, including actual transportation and related
lodging and subsj.stence which is reasonably related to a
tegislitive 
or governmental purpose, or to an issue of
state, national, or international public policy, is not
prohibited or linited by this chapter if either of the
following apply:
(1) The travel is in connection with a speech given
by the elected state officer. 
member of a state board or
commission. or desiqnated emplovee of a state aoencv,
the lodging and subsj.stence expenses are limi.ted to the
day imrnediately precedinq, the day of, and the day im-
rnediately following the speech, and the travel is within
the Uni.ted States.

(c) subdivision (a) applies only to travel, which is

member of a state board or con-

official 
covered by section a72oo. (Section 82019(d).) This

of a state agency, elected state officers, or members of state

advise that a literal 
reading of Sections 89503, 89505 and 89506

4.

As used in this section, rrhonorarj-umrr means a pay-
ment for speaking at any event, participating in a panel
or seminar or engaging in any similar activity. 
For
ourposes of this section, free admission, food, bever-

The provisions of Regulation 18728 underlined above reflect
the provisions emphasized in your letter requesting advice. , Based
upon this emphasii, r{e assume that you are requesting clarifica-
tion concerning the effect of Sections 89504 and 89506 upon
receipt of free admission, subsistence, lodging and travel in con-
necti-on with specified activities of an elected state officer, 
a
member of a stite board or commission or a designated emPloyee of

6 this interin interpretation rnay change as a resuLt of future
regulatory action by the Commission. With respect to your conten-
ti5n as to tfre Comrnission's authority to prornulgate regulations,
we would point out that the Commission is charged with the duty to
Iiberal1y construe the provisions of the Act to acconplislt its
purposes. (Section 81003.) In implementing the Act, it is neces-
sary to look at the goals and intent of the Act as a whoIe, rather
tnan by cLosely scrutinizing the conPonents of whatever definition
may be applicable. 
(In re Witt (1975) 1 FPPC ops. 1, coPy
enllosed.) In watson v. california Fair Political Practices con-
rnission (L99o) 2t7 cal. ApP.3d 1059, the court affirmed the duty
ot tne Comrnission to irnplernent the intent rather than the literal
language of statutes that nake up the Act.

Section 89504 provides as foLlows:
(a) No elected state officer sha1l accept

86203 .
Initiatly, 
we note that these sections do not become effec-
tive until January 1, 1991. Additionally, these sections apply
only to state officials. 
Regulation 18728 would stiI1 apply to
other public officials. 
We further note that Section 89506 only
descriLes palrntents which will not be subject to gift or honoraria
lirnitations. 
rt does not affect disclosure. Thus, if a pa)rment
for travel, lodging, subsistence or free admission is reportable,
section 89506 will not after that requirement.
Regulation 18728 clarifies Section 87207 which sets forth the
disclosure requirernents for income and gifts. 
Regulation 18728
states that certain payments provided to a filer directly in con-
nection with speaking an any event, participating in a panel or
serninar or engaging in any sinilar activity are not trpa)rnentsrl
under the Act and need not be reDorted. (Regulation 18728(a).)


The exemption from the gift and honoraria Linits set forth in
section 89506(a), by its terms, applies only to travel- which is
reported on the recipj-ent's statement of economic interests.
(section 89506(c).) Thus, if certain trave] pa)rments are not
reportable under Section 87207, they are also not subject to the
exemption provided in Section 89506(a).
Therefore, it would appear that Sections 89504 and 89506 do
not affect application of Regul-ati on !8728 as that regulation
interprets Section 87207. If you have specific questions concern-
ing application of these provisions, \re $/i11 be glad to review
them wittr you. Please bear in nind that Sections 89504 and 89506
are not even in effect at present. It is fikely that the Commis-
sion will be adopting regulations.to clarj.fy these provisions in

we agree that every state agency will be required to amend

sections.question:
1. Do PERS board members, officers or employees manage "public investments" within the meaning of the term as used in Section 87200?

2. Are various individuals who are employed by or contract with PERS covered by the meaning of section 87200?

3. Do Sections 89503, 89505 and 89506 apply to individuals (other than members of state boards and commissions) who are subject to the broad reporting requirements of Section 87200?

4. How does the passage of sections 89506 and 89504 affect application of Regulation 18728?
sections.conclusion:
1. PERS board members, officers and employees who manage public employee retirement trust funds are subject to Section 87200 disclosure requirements, as these funds constitute "public moneys" held by state officials in their official capacity.

2. The scope of "other public officials who manage public investments" is pending Commission regulation, but currently, individuals other than designated employees should continue filing on Form 730.

3. Sections 89503, 89505 and 89506 do not apply to PERS officers and employees covered by Section 87200, as these sections only restrict "designated employees" of state agencies, elected state officers, and state board/commission members.

4. [Document truncated - conclusion not available]

sections.question_synthetic: Does the Political Reform Act's disclosure and investment management provisions apply to California Public Employees' Retirement System (PERS) board members and officers, and which honorarium, gift, and travel restrictions apply to these officials?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: PERS officials managing retirement trust funds are covered by Section 87200 disclosure requirements as "public moneys," but honorarium and gift restrictions under Sections 89503-89506 do not apply to them since they are not designated employees, elected officers, or board members. Pending regulations will clarify the scope of covered positions.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Document is truncated at the end (page 6+), missing portions of Issue 4 and likely Issues 5+. Conclusion sections are partially available from the analysis text. Modern format with clear section headings. Informal advice designation noted in opening letter. Some OCR errors present but content is generally clear.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. Do PERS board members, officers or employees manage "public investments" within the meaning of the term as used in Section 87200?

2. Are various individuals who are employed by or contract with PERS covered by the meaning of section 87200?

3. Do Sections 89503, 89505 and 89506 apply to individuals (other than members of state boards and commissions) who are subject to the broad reporting requirements of Section 87200?

4. How does the passage of sections 89506 and 89504 affect application of Regulation 18728?

CONCLUSION: 1. PERS board members, officers and employees who manage public employee retirement trust funds are subject to Section 87200 disclosure requirements, as these funds constitute "public moneys" held by state officials in their official capacity.

2. The scope of "other public officials who manage public investments" is pending Commission regulation, but currently, individuals other than designated employees should continue filing on Form 730.

3. Sections 89503, 89505 and 89506 do not apply to PERS officers and employees covered by Section 87200, as these sections only restrict "designated employees" of state agencies, elected state officers, and state board/commission members.

4. [Document truncated - conclusion not available]

Do PERS board members, officers or employees manage "public investments" erithin the neaninq of the term as used in Section 872 00? In a staff memorandum issued March 22 , ].990, we construed the term trpublic investmentstr to include investments made with ttpubl j-c moneys. rr You suggest that the term ttpubl-ic noneysrr does not include nonies held and invested by PERS, because the funds rnanacred bv PERS are trust funds pursuant to both the California consfitution3 and Section 2o2oo1 . You state that these funds are composed of ernployer and enpl-oyee contributions and the interest thereon and are not public moneys or part of the general fund. our description of public j-nvestroents in the March 22 memorandum as including 'rpublic moneysrt was intended to encompass a wide spectrum of funds within the anbit of the term. A statutory definition of trpublic moneysrr is set forth in Penal code Section 425.5 Typically, that section has been broadly interpreted to include funds received by or held by a public of- ficial in his official capacity. embedding.summary: FPPC provides informal advice to California Public Employees' Retirement System on the application of the Political Reform Act to PERS board members, officers, and employees, particularly regarding disclosure requirements for managing public investments and restrictions on honoraria and gifts.
